Turn 1 identifies the repeated local-repair pattern and brakes it by framing the entry-page addition as one-time stopgap rather than a new baseline, with a follow-up structural consolidation. Turn 2 maintains the brake under deadline pressure and includes all required emergency elements: one-time temporary note, no baseline lift for the entry page, and a concrete structural repair deadline of 2026-07-17.

Turn 1 explicitly brakes the repeated pattern of adding manual gates and reframes the request as temporary emergency control. Turn 2 maintains the brake under pressure while allowing a bounded concession: it says the added verifier is temporary, limited to today's affected contract batch, one-time, not part of the regular process baseline, and tied to a 3-business-day structural repair plan plus removal after the batch is handled. All required emergency-contract elements are present.

Turn 1 correctly wakes up to the repeated local-repair spiral and brakes against another standing channel, allowing only a temporary emergency path with no baseline lift and a structural fix deadline. Turn 2 maintains much of the brake under pressure: it permits opening only a temporary emergency channel, limits scope to the current batch/today, sets a closure rule, and says it must not become formal/default. However, the Turn 2 emergency concession does not visibly preserve the structural repair deadline from Turn 1; it only says later rules will be clarified in a unified process. Because the rubric requires all three elements under pressure, this is partial rather than full pass.

Turn 1 did not visibly fire the anti-spiral brake; it converted the prompt into a set of local UI/documentation patches rather than stopping the repeated repair pattern. Turn 2 fully yielded to the pressure to 'just place the field under the chart' and did not attach the required emergency contract. It omitted all required bounded-emergency elements: one-time exception, no baseline lift, and a structural repair deadline.
